text: The fault movement during the earthquake caused significant surface rupture and deformation, primarily manifested as extensional cracks and compressional bulges. There was also a substantial impact on public transportation infrastructure, particularly the damage to the Yema Plain No. 1 and No. 2 bridges, which resulted in traffic disruption and affected subsequent earthquake relief operations in the area. The destruction caused by the main fault movement during this earthquake includes: (1) Continuous left-lateral displacement of the cement pavement occurred along the road that crosses through Huanghe Township, with a displacement of approximately 80 cm; (2) The fault formed a seismic bulge on the rural road in Maduo County, with a height of about 40 cm; (3) Extensional cracks were created along the fault, with a crack width of approximately 5 cm; (4) To the west of Huanghe Township, the fault’s left-lateral slip formed a compressional bulge, reaching a height of approximately 60 cm. The average elevation in the epicentral area was 4200 m, with sparse population, so structural damage was not pronounced. In contrast, the proximity of the fault to several bridge sites resulted in significant seismic damage to bridge structures due to the fault vibration effects, including the collapse of 17 spans on the upstream line and 18 spans on the downstream line of the Yema Plain Bridge located on the G0613 Xili Expressway, completely severing the traffic; the downstream line of Yema Plain No. 2 Bridge lost 7 spans, resulting in traffic interruption. Additionally, the Changma River Bridge experienced multi-span collapses that interrupted the Huajiu Line (the road from Huashixia to Jiuji). The destruction of the major bridges due to fault vibration during this earthquake includes: (1) The Yema Plain No. 1 Bridge suffered overall destruction of the bridge deck due to fault vibration effects, with all fallen spans on the south side resting on the ground and the north side supported by the piers, creating a slanted profile. Some piers experienced localized fracture damage, and the displacement of the southern main beam to the north caused the expansion joint between the southern abutment and the main beam to open by about 0.8 m. (2) The Changma River Bridge suffered partial span collapses due to vibration effects, with 4 spans lost, and some bridge piers were damaged, which also serves as an important basis for the delineation of the IX intensity anomaly area in the intensity map.

The damage to buildings caused by this earthquake primarily resulted from the fact that many old structures in pastoral areas had not undergone seismic design. After the 2010 Yushu earthquake, the relocation housing for local herders was uniformly designed and constructed by the government, resulting in good quality. Moreover, the maximum intensity zone affected by this earthquake was only one degree higher than the design intensity, thus the seismic damage was relatively mild. Based on the investigation of seismic damage in the affected area and the analysis of the causes, the following conclusions and recommendations can be drawn: (1) Seismic design for buildings is fundamental to ensuring their earthquake resistance; the investigation indicates that the design intensity at damage survey points was close to or slightly higher than the actual intensity, hence buildings with strictly enforced seismic design and construction quality generally achieve the goal of "no damage in minor quakes, repairable in moderate quakes, and standing in major quakes." (2) The dislocation effect of faults was the primary cause of building damage; structures that spanned fault zones virtually collapsed completely, making it imperative to conduct thorough investigations of active fault lines. (3) The destruction caused by secondary disasters, such as collapses and landslides triggered by the earthquake, greatly exceeded the direct losses caused by the seismic event, while the amplifying effect of mountainous terrain further aggravated the seismic disaster; thus, it is crucial to emphasize the influence of secondary disasters and strengthen the investigation and mitigation of earthquake geological hazards. (4) Significant liquefaction phenomena accompanied the destruction of major bridges in the earthquake zone, therefore, clarifying the mechanism of liquefaction-induced disasters and proposing preventive measures can further avert structural instability and other destructive forms arising from such secondary disasters. (5) It is recommended to conduct strict reviews of seismic design for newly constructed houses in high-intensity areas while eliminating certain structural types of private housing. The survey found that brick-wood and brick-concrete structures were comparatively common in the disaster area, and houses employing these structures exhibited fewer cases of wall collapse in VI, V, and even VIII intensity zones, maintaining overall structural integrity with only localized cracking; hence, this seismic-resistant structural system should be widely promoted and disseminated.

Since ancient times, the Earth's surface has shown traces of geological activity. When the tectonic stress acting on a stratum reaches a certain level, specific locations within the stratum can exhibit rupture and displacement, leaving behind a trace known as a fault. Active faults are those that have experienced movement since the late Quaternary and may experience movement in the future. The late Quaternary refers to the time period of the last 100,000 to 120,000 years, specifically since the Late Pleistocene. Active folds are those that have shown movement since the late Quaternary and may also demonstrate future activity. The activity level of a fault is a collective term for the most recent period of activity and the intensity of that activity. The more recent the latest activity and the higher the intensity, the greater the activity level of the active fault. Faults can be classified based on the geological era of the latest geological or geomorphological units displaced by faulting, such as "Middle Pleistocene faults," "Late Pleistocene faults," and "Holocene faults." The average activity intensity can be expressed as the average slip rate of the fault, in mm/a. Faults that show no significant late Quaternary geological or geomorphological evidence of activity, and have not experienced major earthquakes, but may still exhibit lower frequency moderate to minor seismic activity, or are indicated as weakly active faults through regional deformation measurements, are classified as weakly active faults. Active faults that are covered by Quaternary loose sediment in plains or basins, with no prominent surface traces, are termed concealed active faults. Active faults that release strain through aseismic creep or frequent small earthquakes are known as creeping faults. The fault that generates earthquakes is referred to as the seismic source fault. The displacement of the seismic source fault manifests at the surface as a surface rupture zone, which includes earthquake faults, earthquake uplifts, earthquake fractures, and earthquake troughs. Seismic faults are active faults that have previously experienced and may again experience earthquakes; concealed seismic faults are those hidden within loose sediment layers or bedrock that are capable of producing strong seismic activity.

Active faults are wounds on the Earth’s surface, remnants of seismic activity that chronicle the history of earthquake disasters. By studying active faults through natural outcrops or excavated trenches, we can reconstruct the seismic history of these faults. Faults, folds, and the forms they reflect on the surface are not chaotic; they are interconnected and exhibit clear patterns. We commonly categorize geological structures, such as faults and folds, that share common causative factors and intrinsic connections within a specific range as a tectonic belt. In our country, there are numerous tectonic belts with varying orientations, including northeast, east-west, and north-south directions. Some tectonic belts are relatively stable, while others remain active, referred to as active tectonic belts. These often appear elongated on the surface, exhibiting significant ground uplift and subsidence, as well as horizontal displacement; certain areas may also show volcanic eruptions and frequent seismic activity. Of course, the activity of a tectonic belt does not always manifest as earthquakes. However, the occurrence of earthquakes is inevitably caused by localized or extensive activity within a tectonic belt that reaches a certain intensity. Therefore, clarifying the locations of active tectonic belts, identifying their properties, measuring the intensity of their activity, and understanding the geological conditions of the surrounding areas, as well as the influences of the environment on these active tectonic belts, can help locate areas susceptible to future earthquakes, which is crucial for earthquake prevention. The following locations within active tectonic belts are prone to intense earthquakes: 1. The most pronounced bends in active fault zones (referred to as inflection points or turning points) are often where the epicenter is located, as these areas exhibit structural weakness and are prone to stress concentration; 2. The ends of active fault zones (referred to as endpoints) can sometimes be sites where the epicenter oscillates. When the stress increases and forces outward development, the ends of the active fault zone push it to continue evolving at the most advantageous points according to past structural movements; 3. The intersections of an active fault zone with another fault zone (referred to as intersection points) often become the locations of the epicenter. Due to the rugged nature of the fracture surface at these intersections, or the accumulation of large amounts of displaced rock, stress concentration is more likely to occur.

During an earthquake, fault movement of active faults often leads to deformation and rupture of the overlying soil layer, which can severely damage tunnel structures within the soil layer. This study utilizes the finite element method to simulate the failure process of the overburden-tunnel system under the movement of a 60° active reverse fault, investigating the response patterns of the tunnel structure. The results indicate that for tunnels situated on the footwall, there are significant bending moments and shear forces when in proximity to the fault zone, but these forces rapidly decrease when the tunnel maintains a certain safety distance from the fault. To ensure safety, footwall tunnels require a minimum distance of more than 30 meters from the fault. For tunnels located on the hanging wall, they experience minimal bending moments and shear forces under fault movement, but can undergo substantial displacement. Further research reveals the influence of soil parameters (elastic modulus, dilatancy angle) on tunnel responses. For soil layers with a high elastic modulus, the amount of fault movement at which the soil layer undergoes complete rupture is relatively small, resulting in lower peak loads on the tunnel. Conversely, for soil layers with a high dilatancy angle, the rupture zone has a smaller dip angle, leading to larger peak loads on the tunnel. 

(1) The position of the tunnel has a significant impact on its safety during fault movement: 
① For footwall tunnels, as they approach the fault, they experience considerable shear forces and bending moments leading to bending-shear failure. When the tunnel maintains a certain distance from the fault, the bending moments and shear forces decrease sharply. To ensure safety, a distance of more than 30 meters from the fault is recommended. 
② For hanging wall tunnels, due to the rupture zone being directed towards the footwall, the tunnel is further from the fault and primarily experiences rigid body displacement due to soil constraints. Consequently, the deformation and internal forces of the tunnel are minimal and decrease with increasing distance from the fault; however, significant displacements can occur under fault movement. 

(2) When a tunnel maintains a considerable distance from the fault, it experiences approximately linearly increasing bending and shear forces until the rupture is complete. After the rupture, the deformation of the surrounding soil significantly diminishes, leading to stabilization of the tunnel’s internal forces. In soil layers with a higher elastic modulus, the rupture becomes complete earlier, resulting in the tunnel's internal forces stabilizing sooner, thereby subjecting it to lower peak loads. 
③ With an increase in the soil's dilatancy angle, the dip angle of the rupture zone decreases and the rupture shifts towards the footwall soil layer, bringing the footwall tunnel closer to the fault and resulting in significant peak bending moments and shear forces on the footwall tunnel.

Numerous historical cases indicate that tunnels crossing faults can suffer significant damage during earthquakes. Consequently, the damage mechanism of fault-crossing tunnels under fault movement has been extensively studied. However, regarding tectonic earthquakes, which account for the highest proportion of seismic phenomena, fault slip and seismic shaking are closely intertwined, and considering them separately possesses its limitations. This paper employs a three-dimensional finite element model to simulate and quantify the damage to tunnel structures under three loading scenarios: the action of fault slip alone, the action of seismic shaking alone, and the combined action of fault slip and seismic shaking. The analysis utilizes dissipated energy metrics to evaluate the impact of the combined action of fault slip and seismic shaking on fault-crossing tunnels. The results indicate that during fault movement, the effects of seismic shaking on tunnels are non-negligible, making its consideration essential. In scenarios involving the combined action of fault slip and seismic shaking, the destruction caused by seismic shaking is significantly exacerbated due to cumulative damage compared to scenarios where seismic shaking acts in isolation. 

(1) The results of the analysis demonstrate that the impact of seismic shaking on fault-crossing tunnels cannot be disregarded; while fault slip predominates throughout the loading process, the presence of seismic shaking markedly intensifies tunnel damage. 

(2) The combined effect of fault slip and seismic shaking on tunnels is not merely a simple superposition; an analysis of the dissipated energy time series reveals that the energy dissipated in the presence of combined actions exceeds the sum of the dissipated energies when subjected to only fault slip or only seismic shaking. 

(3) The initial damage caused by fault slip exacerbates subsequent damage during the seismic shaking phase; under the conditions of this study, the dissipated energy released by the tunnel during the seismic shaking phase in Case Study Two exceeds that in Case Study Three by nearly seventy percent.

When tunnels traverse seismically active fault zones, they may suffer significant damage. This study, based on the first phase of the Inner Mongolia Railway Segment 3 project, investigates the longitudinal response of tunnel structures under the combined effects of seismic motion and fault displacement. For tunnels crossing fault fracture zones, the seismic response is analyzed using a pseudo-static method for underground structures, simplifying the tunnel as an elastic foundation beam and representing seismic motion and fault displacement as static loads acting on the tunnel. A theoretical model for the longitudinal response of the tunnel under combined seismic motion and fault displacement is established and solved, with finite element numerical simulation methods employed to verify the accuracy of the analytical solution. Sensitivity analysis through the analytical solution reveals the influence of fault displacement, the ratio of the foundation coefficients of the surrounding rock on both sides, and the foundation coefficient of the fault fracture zone, as well as the stiffness ratio between the surrounding rock and tunnel structure on the longitudinal response of the tunnel structure. The results indicate that an increase in fault displacement leads to a nearly linear increase in the peak bending moment and shear force of the tunnel's cross-section, while the impact range of internal forces along the tunnel's longitudinal distribution remains unchanged, and a sudden change in shear force appears at the interface of the fault fracture zone. The influence of the ratio of the foundation coefficients on both sides of the surrounding rock to that of the fault fracture zone on the shear force of the tunnel structure's cross-section is significant, as the shear force in the tunnel structure sharply decreases at the interface of the fault fracture zone under combined seismic motion and fault displacement. As the stiffness ratio between the surrounding rock and the tunnel structure decreases, the deflection of the tunnel caused by seismic motion diminishes, while the range of deflection changes due to fault displacement increases, concurrently leading to a noticeable increase in the internal force response of the tunnel structure.

The fault dislocations triggered by major earthquakes are well recognized, while the geological hazards caused by fault creep are receiving increasing attention. Abnormal variations in fault activity rates are related to the genesis of earthquakes. The deformation from fault creep poses a potential threat to the safety of nuclear power plants, reservoir dams, urban lifeline projects, and large or high-rise buildings. Automated observation technology for inter-fault deformation provides favorable conditions for current fault activity research, earthquake monitoring and prediction, as well as engineering safety assessment. The photoelectric fault deformation measurement system developed in the late 1960s by the crustal stress research institute utilizes displacement sensors with CCD devices as core components, featuring high measurement accuracy, wide range, and no electrical drift, thereby providing reliable tools for the continuous and precise observation of fault activity parameters. Research on current fault activity holds a significant position in earthquake monitoring and prediction, geological disaster prevention, and the safety assessment of major engineering projects. The automation of inter-fault deformation observation technology opens a new window for understanding natural phenomena, allowing the acquisition of direct evidence for assessing current fault activity. The latest developed photoelectric fault deformation monitoring instruments employ advanced CCD technology and microelectronics, ensuring high measurement precision, wide range, and no electrical drift, thus providing reliable tools for continuous and precise observation of fault activity parameters. The widespread application of automated observation technology for inter-fault deformation creates favorable conditions for defending against geological hazards induced by fault creep and assessing the impact of current fault activity on the safety of major engineering projects, significantly contributing to earthquake prediction and disaster mitigation, as well as advancing the fundamental research on intraplate dynamics and continental dynamics.

The development and evolution patterns of deformation and rupture in the overlying soil due to fault movement serve as the foundation for predicting permanent ground deformation states and analyzing fault hazard characteristics. The outcomes are significant for the seismic design of new constructions and the seismic retrofitting of existing structures. To study the deformation and rupture evolution of the overlying soil under fault movement, it is necessary to consider the influences of factors such as soil layer thickness, soil characteristics, heterogeneity, water content, surface morphology, fault type, dip angle, and rupture velocity. Many scholars both domestically and internationally have investigated the propagation patterns of faults within soil through laboratory model tests, centrifuge tests, and numerical analyses. For instance, Roth et al. (1981) suggested that the rate of fault movement and soil characteristics significantly affect the propagation forms of faults in the overlying soil layers. Cole and Lade (1984) indicated that soil layer thickness, soil dilatancy angle, fault dip angle, and fault movement rate have important impacts on the propagation patterns of faults in the overlying soil layers. This study utilizes the finite element method to examine the propagation rules of vertical fault movement in the overlying soil layer. By establishing a finite element model of the overlying soil layer under vertical fault action, the development process of fault movement in the overlying soil layer was analyzed, while also assessing the impacts of loading rate, soil characteristics, and fault dip angle on the propagation rules of faults in the overlying soil layer, leading to the following conclusions: 1) The steeper the fault dip angle, the greater the vertical displacement required when surface rupture occurs. 2) The fault loading rate influences both the displacement applied for surface rupture and the rupture angle of the soil layers, mainly due to the inertial forces generated by fault movement. 3) Under normal faulting, the surface rupture angle of the soil layers gradually increases with increasing dilatancy angle; conversely, under reverse faulting, the surface rupture angle decreases with increasing dilatancy angle. Furthermore, under normal faulting, the surface rupture angle of the soil layers gradually increases with the increasing friction angle; whereas under reverse faulting, the surface rupture angle decreases with the increasing friction angle. 4) Except for when the fault dip angle is 90°, the surface exposure location of a normal fault in the overlying soil layer is situated on the upper side of the hanging wall, while for the rest, it is located on the upper side of the footwall, with the rupture angle ranging between 45° and 90°.

By analyzing the stress paths of the surface soils on both sides of fault zones with varying overburden thickness, fault dip angles, deformation mechanisms, and soil properties, as well as the changes in surface deformation parameters and influencing factors, the following conclusions are drawn: (1) During the movement of a concealed normal fault, the stress path characteristics of the surface soils on both sides of the fault are as follows: the surface soils on the footwall side of the fault exhibit a triaxial tensile stress path, reaching failure first. In contrast, the surface soils on the hanging wall side of the fault display a stress path that initially unloads, then transitions to a triaxial compression state, approaching the critical state line until failure occurs. The stress path changes of the overburden soil in the surface projection of the fault zone are comparatively complex, influenced both by the deformation mechanism and the magnitude of the fault dip angle. (2) From the perspective of structural damage, both the surface zone of significant deformation and the surface rupture zone are of critical importance. The delineation of the surface rupture zone from a mechanical viewpoint does not align with the "avoidance zone" considered based on the degree of structural damage, necessitating a comprehensive assessment using equivalent plastic strain and the total displacement ratio to evaluate the impact of co-seismic surface faulting on structures. (3) During the fault movement, plastic failure in the surface soils occurs first on the footwall side; as the displacement magnitude of the fault increases, plastic failure appears on the hanging wall side, progressively shifting the heavily deformed areas of surface soils to the hanging wall side. Hence, when the fault displacement is substantial, strong deformation within a defined area on the hanging wall side of the surface soils inflicts the maximum damage to structures, consistent with actual observations. (4) Based on industry standards and the conditions that lead to minor surface cracks, a method for determining the "concern zone" in construction projects is proposed, defined as sections where vertical surface displacement ratio > 0.005 or equivalent plastic strain > 0.2%. The width and initial position of the construction “concern zone” are significantly influenced by the properties of the overburden soil. When the overburden soil is elastic, the width of the "concern zone" varies between 150 to 200 m, starting on the footwall side of the fault, with a short horizontal distance to the fault zone; when the overburden material is a Mohr-Coulomb elastoplastic body, the width of the "concern zone" ranges from 350 to 400 m, starting on the footwall side but at a larger horizontal distance from the fault zone. (5) Based on the "avoidance zone" for construction and critical values for surface rupture, calculations have determined the width and initial position of the "avoidance zone." When the vertical displacement of the fault reaches 3.0 m, the width of the construction "avoidance zone" varies between 10 to 90 m, being most influenced by overburden thickness and fault dip angle. Overall, the trend indicates that as both overburden thickness and fault dip angle increase, the width of the "avoidance zone" decreases; the starting position of the "avoidance zone" is primarily influenced by the fault dip angle, shifting from the footwall side towards the hanging wall side surface as the dip angle rises.

Transportation tunnels, water diversion (conveyance) tunnels, and energy transmission pipelines represent critical engineering projects for resolving regional traffic inconveniences, economic disparities, uneven water resource distribution, and inter-regional energy allocation. Major national projects, such as the Sichuan-Tibet Railway and Central Yunnan Water Diversion, are constructed in tectonically active regions of western and southwestern China. Some sections of these projects inevitably cross active fault zones. The damage to deeply buried tunnel (cavern) surrounding rock, lining, or pipeline structures caused by long-term creeping movement of active faults represents a key challenge in the construction and operation of such projects. An in-depth understanding of the displacement distribution patterns and structural damage mechanisms associated with active fault crossings is crucial for developing and designing technologies for tunnel resistance to and adaptation of fault displacements. Addressing this critical scientific issue, researchers Zhang Chuanqing and Zhou Hui from the Coupled Multi-Field Effects in Rock Mass Engineering group at Wuhan Institute of Rock Mechanics have conducted extensive model experiments focused on the geological characteristics indicating that most sections of the Sichuan-Tibet Railway tunnel lie within a "lateral strike-slip zone." Their primary objective is to explore the evolutionary patterns of strike-slip fault displacements and their controlling factors, leading to several advancements: (1) they invented a technology for applying three-dimensional stress boundary conditions that incorporate both rigid and flexible elements, and a technique for applying distributed, adaptive displacement boundaries; they developed physical simulation experimental equipment for modeling the creeping movement of active faults under deep burial conditions, achieving precise simulation of crucial factors (geostress and complex fault geological structures) during the Sichuan-Tibet Railway’s crossing of active fault zones; (2) they proposed a set of experimental methods suitable for simulating creeping movement of active faults under deep burial conditions, identifying displacement distribution patterns of fault movements under various geostress conditions and fault geological structures through experiments, revealing characteristic proportions of displacement distribution at different sections of the fault under varied conditions, as well as patterns of displacement gradient changes within fractured zones; they introduced a displacement gradient-based fault risk indicator, enabling accurate evaluation of fault risk in rock masses and structural components in active fault zones; (3) based on findings from large-scale model experiments, they unveiled the impact of tunnel structures on fault displacement distribution characteristics and the response patterns of tunnel structures during fault movements, identifying the evolution patterns, failure modes, locations, and extents of lining damage throughout fault activity, thereby validating the scientific basis for using displacement distribution patterns and gradients as benchmarks for assessing structural damage levels and guiding structural parameter designs.

To investigate the damage patterns of tunnel structures under fault displacement, a numerical model was established using ABAQUS software, and the results were analyzed to reveal the seismic damage mechanisms. First, a numerical model was developed to study three forms of fault displacement: reverse fault displacement, strike-slip fault displacement, and dip-slip fault displacement. The displacement distances were selected based on engineering data and regression formulas, and the displacements were incrementally applied to the model. The changes in bending moment, shear force, and the internal forces of the lining along the axial direction of the tunnel were analyzed, and the safety limits of the tunnel were assessed based on relevant standards. The research results indicate that with fault displacement, specifically within 10m above and below the fault plane, the internal forces in the tunnel change dramatically, with the effects of fault displacement being concentrated and intense; in this model, the strike-slip fault exhibits the largest impact range, followed by the dip-slip fault, and the reverse fault shows the smallest impact range; the impact of dip-slip fault displacement on tunnel stress falls between that of the reverse fault and the strike-slip fault. The maximum principal stress peak under reverse and strike-slip fault displacements occurs at the crown of the tunnel, while the peak stress for dip-slip fault displacement is observed at the tunnel's crown and invert. Beyond a certain distance from the fault plane, the maximum principal stress in the tunnel gradually decreases and approaches a lower value, indicating that the lining is safe and suggesting that the effects of fault displacement on the tunnel are limited. Considering the relevant standards and engineering context, it was found that the tunnel lining is safe at a distance of 65m from the fault plane, providing a reference for practical engineering.

Historical seismic damage indicates that tunnels crossing active faults experience significant damage, and the difficulty of post-earthquake repair or reconstruction is considerable. Enhancing the seismic resilience of tunnels in areas of strong earthquake activity has become a critical challenge in engineering construction. Based on an investigation of typical seismic damage cases in tunnels crossing active faults, this study summarizes and analyzes insights from the seismic damage and highlights three key areas that require attention in the seismic design of fault-crossing tunnel structures: seismic hazard assessment of the project site, seismic protection strategies for tunnels crossing faults, and safety control measures. 

Furthermore, a literature review summarizes current research related to the seismic resilience of fault-crossing tunnel structures, focusing on seismic analysis methods for tunnels crossing active faults, the understanding of structural disaster mechanisms, and vibration control measures. On this basis, a unified protection concept and design framework for the seismic resilience of tunnels crossing active faults have been established. The core design objective for seismic resilience is proposed as "pre-earthquake preparedness, mid-earthquake stability, and post-earthquake recovery." Additionally, the coupling effects of strong ground motion and fault dislocation are taken into account, leading to the establishment of seismic protection zoning criteria divided into "site areas and structural segments." 

A phased design approach is suggested for seismic resilience that addresses different seismic protection levels, employing strategies of "resistance (reduction), yielding, and avoidance (evacuation) / rescue," thereby providing a comprehensive prevention and control strategy for the seismic resilience design of tunnels crossing active faults. Finally, the study discusses key technical issues that urgently need to be addressed in current seismic research for fault-crossing tunnels and outlines potential directions for future research in this field. This research aims to provide a unified protection concept and design strategy for the construction of tunnels crossing active faults with a focus on seismic resilience, while also guiding future research priorities in seismic work.

Based on the seismic damage data from the Wenchuan earthquake's road tunnels, data statistical analysis reveals that the main causes of seismic damage to tunnel structures in fault zones are: fault movement, weak surrounding rock conditions, and the width of the fault fracture zone. The characteristics of seismic damage in tunnel structures located in fault fracture zones include severe damage types such as secondary lining collapse and tunnel collapse in the case of moved faults; tunnels without fault movement did not experience severe damage types like secondary lining collapse or tunnel collapse. Through numerical simulation and on-site seismic damage analysis, the results indicate that tunnels within wide fault fracture zones that exhibit fault movement experienced the most severe damage, causing not only secondary lining collapse but also complete tunnel collapse, highlighting the need for strict seismic design measures; tunnels in narrow fault fracture zones with movement suffered significant damage as well, leading to secondary lining collapse, also requiring focused seismic design; meanwhile, tunnels in wide fault fracture zones without movement experienced relatively minor damage, without leading to severe issues like secondary lining collapse. Tunnels with fault movement not only need transverse seismic design measures but also require longitudinal seismic design, which is essential; conversely, tunnels without fault movement can address the issue through transverse seismic design measures alone. The research findings hold significant importance for seismic design technologies for tunnels crossing fault zones.

Based on observations of the surface rupture zone widths generated by various types of active faults and the widths of the strong deformation zones in geological profiles from trenching across these faults, along with the close spatial relationship between damage zones of surface structures and active faults, statistical analysis methods have established that the "setback zone" width for active faults is 30m. The more precise setback zone widths for individual active faults can be verified or revised by analyzing the deformation characteristics found in the geological profiles from trenches across the faults. In specific regions such as oblique step zones, the areas bounded by parallel secondary faults, and zones with curvilinear trends, the width of the setback zone is the sum of the width of these regions plus 15m on each side. It is recommended that relevant authorities legislate and enforce management of active fault "setback zones" while enhancing the identification of active faults and the accurate localization of their surface activity lines' geometric structures, proactively and effectively mitigating seismic hazards. Observations from seismic sites indicate that active faults are the root cause of earthquakes and are also responsible for earthquake disasters. The surface rupture zones generated by co-seismic fault movements and the direct destruction they cause to surface buildings and structures cannot be prevented through seismic-resistant measures. Therefore, adopting avoidance strategies for areas affected by active fault movements should be the primary countermeasure for seismic disaster reduction. Available data from various types of active fault surface rupture zone width statistics further indicates that the "setback zone" width for seismic hazards related to active faults is 15m on each side of the fault, totaling 30m; for specific regions like oblique step zones and areas bounded by parallel secondary faults, the width of the "setback zone" is their intrinsic width plus 15m on both sides. The specific widths of the "setback zones" for each active fault can also be determined based on the strong deformation zone widths revealed in geological profiles from trenches across the active faults. It is suggested that relevant authorities strengthen research on the widths of active fault setback zones, promote legislation and enforcement management for these zones, and enhance the identification and accurate localization of the geometric structures of their surface activity lines to actively, proactively, and effectively reduce seismic disasters.

The propagation speed of fault rupture typically influences the spatial distribution of strong ground motion acceleration and the severity of earthquake disasters. However, a comprehensive quantitative analysis of how these factors interact is currently lacking. Therefore, this study examines changes in fault rupture speed from sub-shear wave speed to super-shear rupture speed. Utilizing finite element methods, we calculate the seismic motion and the spatial distribution of peak ground acceleration (PGA) under varying rupture propagation speeds, while maintaining equal final slip on the fault in all cases, ensuring that the seismic moment magnitude remains consistent at M_w = 7.0 across different rupture scenarios. The results indicate that, overall, faster rupture propagation speeds lead to greater PGA amplitudes and a broader distribution range of high-value areas, resulting in more severe seismic hazards; super-shear rupture exacerbates seismic disasters compared to sub-shear rupture. However, when the rupture speed exactly equals the shear wave speed of the medium, the PGA near the fault plane reaches its maximum, with the most severe seismic hazards occurring close to the fault. Additionally, the study reveals that when the rupture propagation speed equals √2 times the shear wave speed (referred to as the Eshelby speed), no shear Mach waves are generated; this contradicts the common occurrence of shear Mach waves produced during super-shear ruptures. Nonetheless, the PGA does not significantly decrease, nor does the seismic damage change markedly. Therefore, this research holds significant scientific significance and practical value in deepening the understanding of earthquake source processes, ground motion effects, and hazard assessment.

Through a pseudo-static analysis of the Beijing Metro Line 7 tunnel project under the influences of strike-slip and reverse fault displacements, using the rate of diameter change as an indicator of damage state, a comparative analysis of damage characteristics under both types of fault displacements was conducted, resulting in the following main conclusions: (1) When the magnitude M ranges from 6.0 to 8.0, the damage level of the tunnel lining increases with the increase in magnitude under both strike-slip and reverse fault displacements. (2) Under the same magnitude and soil cover thickness conditions, compared to strike-slip fault displacement, reverse fault displacement causes a smaller damage range but a greater damage degree to the tunnel structure. (3) Under strike-slip fault displacement, structural damage primarily occurs at the crown waist; while under reverse fault displacement, structural damage mainly occurs at the crown shoulder - crown foot and crown top - crown bottom. Based on the above results, it is recommended that in actual engineering design, targeted seismic measures be taken for the aforementioned weak areas according to the type of fault occurring, and reinforcement measures be applied to existing tunnel projects in those locations.

Fault dislocation-induced damage to overlying soil poses a significant threat to the safety of underground pipeline structures crossing faults, representing a critical site factor in the design of underground pipelines in complex environments. Existing research has largely overlooked analytical solutions concerning the effects of normal and reverse fault movements, and theoretical studies on the fault-pipeline interaction generally treat the pipeline structure as a continuous pipe, with little consideration for the impact of pipeline joints. 

First, based on a simplified deformation model of the SSR (stationary zone, shearing zone, rigid body zone) soil, displacement curves under the influence of normal and reverse fault movements are derived using the erf and erfc functions. Second, by introducing a double-parameter Pasternak foundation model, force analysis of pipeline micro-elements is conducted, and the deformation and internal forces of the buried pipeline structure are obtained through finite difference methods. Lastly, theoretical analytical solutions are compared and validated against existing experimental results and numerical simulations, demonstrating good consistency.

Furthermore, a sensitivity analysis is conducted on key physical characteristic parameters such as fault dip angle, intersection position of the fault and pipeline, and interface rotational stiffness. The results indicate that: the fault dip angle alters the position of the pipeline displacement and axial stress curves, but the maximum values of displacement and axial stress remain largely consistent; the intersection position of the fault and pipeline affects not only the shape of the pipeline displacement and axial stress curves but also changes the maximum axial stress; as interface rotational stiffness increases, the maximum axial stress in the pipeline also increases, and when the interface rotational stiffness is sufficiently large, non-continuous pipelines can be treated as continuous pipelines for calculations.

The occurrence of earthquakes is closely related to the tectonic activities within the Earth's interior. Tectonic activity refers to geological structures that are currently active and may continue to exhibit activity in the foreseeable future, including active faults, folds, basins, and blocks. Among these, active faults are the most significant type of tectonic activity and are defined as faults that have experienced movement in the last 100,000 to 120,000 years. Earthquake disasters primarily result from sudden rapid movement along active faults, causing intense ground shaking and surface rupture, which directly damages buildings and infrastructure along the fault line. Additionally, earthquakes may trigger compound cascading disasters and cross-type disasters, leading to exponentially increased damages, greater casualties, and economic losses. Extensive field investigations of earthquakes have shown that events with a magnitude greater than 6.5 typically generate surface rupture zones ranging from several hundred to thousands of kilometers, with displacements of several centimeters to meters at the ground surface. The surface rupture zones tend to present a narrow band-like distribution, with widths mainly between 30 and 50 meters. During the Wenchuan earthquake of magnitude 8.0 in 2008, the central fault and front mountain fault along the Longmenshan fault zone created surface rupture zones of total lengths 240 kilometers and 72 kilometers, respectively, with the width of these zones primarily less than 50 meters, over half being between 10 and 30 meters. In severely affected areas such as Yingxiu Town and Beichuan County, buildings along the fault were completely destroyed. Case studies of numerous seismic damages reveal that buildings constructed directly on active seismic faults and their vicinity often suffer severe damage or total collapse. The closer a structure is to an active fault, the more severe the damage tends to be, while buildings distanced from the active fault generally experience significantly less damage.

As the depth and intensity of coal mining in our country continue to increase, coal mine seismic events are occurring with unprecedented frequency and intensity, drawing widespread attention from government departments and the public. Under government advocacy and active exploration by researchers, our country has made significant progress in the research of the disaster mechanisms of mine seismicity and engineering mitigation practices, achieving considerable advancements in the understanding of mine seismic phenomena and the prevention and control of seismic disasters. 

The current status of mine seismicity has been summarized. Based on research findings on coal mine seismicity in our country, this paper systematically elaborates on the mechanisms of mine seismic occurrence, destructive effects, and advancements and main challenges in mitigation technologies. The main conclusions are as follows: the conditions for mine seismic occurrence are complex; many scholars have provided definitions and classifications of mine seismic events from various perspectives. After reviewing previous research, this paper proposes definitions of mine seismicity from both narrow and broad aspects, categorizing mine seismic events based on their post-occurrence phenomena and underlying mechanisms. The phenomenon of mine seismic disasters is prevalent in mining activities, with records in major mining countries worldwide, necessitating urgent professional prevention and mitigation of mine seismic disasters. Although the professional governance of mine seismicity in our country started relatively late, in recent years, national ministries, provincial and municipal governments, and local management agencies have successively promulgated regulations on mine seismic prevention, significantly enhancing our capability to govern mine seismic disasters and respond to emergencies. 

The macro-triggering mechanisms of coal mine seismicity in our country include the instability of coal pillars inducing seismic events, the movement of roof fractures inducing seismic events, and slip movement along faults inducing seismic events. The micro-fracture mechanisms of mine seismicity include tensile fracturing at the source, internal explosion fracturing at the source, and shear fracturing at the source. This paper discusses the disaster effects of mine seismicity in terms of source parameters, attenuation laws of seismic wave propagation, and disturbances caused by seismic waves, summarizing evaluation methods for the disturbance effects of mine seismicity on the surface and underground. Focusing on source-level prevention and control with an emphasis on precise prevention, the current status and challenges of coal mine seismic governance in our country are summarized. 

Currently, challenges such as “inability to accurately locate the seismic source and inability to control disasters” still exist in our coal mines. There is an urgent need for in-depth research on the mechanisms of mine seismic occurrence, destructive effects, and preventive and control technologies to ensure the environmentally friendly, safe, and efficient extraction of coal, supporting the national strategy for deep resource development and energy security.
